## NEURAL TIME-DEPENDENT PARTIAL DIFFERENTIAL EQUATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Partial differential equations (PDEs) play a crucial role in studying a vast number of problems in science and engineering. Numerically solving nonlinear and/or highdimensional PDEs is frequently a challenging task. Inspired by the traditional finite difference and finite elements methods and emerging advancements in machine learning, we propose a sequence-to-sequence learning (Seq2Seq) framework called Neural-PDE, which allows one to automatically learn governing rules of any timedependent PDE system from existing data by using a bidirectional LSTM encoder, and predict the solutions in next _n_ time steps. One critical feature of our proposed framework is that the Neural-PDE is able to simultaneously learn and simulate all variables of interest in a PDE system. We test the Neural-PDE by a range of examples, from one-dimensional PDEs to a multi-dimensional and nonlinear complex fluids model. The results show that the Neural-PDE is capable of learning the initial conditions, boundary conditions and differential operators defining the initial-boundary-value problem of a PDE system without the knowledge of the specific form of the PDE system. In our experiments, the Neural-PDE can efficiently extract the dynamics within 20 epochs training and produce accurate predictions. Furthermore, unlike the traditional machine learning approaches for learning PDEs, such as CNN and MLP, which require great quantity of parameters for model precision, the Neural-PDE shares parameters among all time steps, and thus considerably reduces computational complexity and leads to a fast learning algorithm.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The research of time-dependent partial differential equations (PDEs) is regarded as one of the most important disciplines in applied mathematics. PDEs appear ubiquitously in a broad spectrum of fields including physics, biology, chemistry, and finance, to name a few. Despite their fundamental importance, most PDEs can not be solved analytically and have to rely on numerical solving methods. Developing efficient and accurate numerical schemes for solving PDEs, therefore, has been an active research area over the past few decades (Courant et al., 1967; Osher & Sethian, 1988; LeVeque; Cockburn et al., 2012; Thomas, 2013; Johnson, 2012). Still, devising stable and accurate schemes with acceptable computational cost is a difficult task, especially when nonlinear and(or) high-dimensional PDEs are considered. Additionally, PDE models emerged from science and engineering disciplines usually require huge empirical data for model calibration and validation, and determining the multidimensional parameters in such a PDE system poses another challenge (Peng et al., 2020).

Deep learning is considered to be the state-of-the-art tool in classification and prediction of nonlinear inputs, such as image, text, and speech (Litjens et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2018; LeCun et al., 1998; Krizhevsky et al., 2012; Hinton et al., 2012). Recently, considerable efforts have been made to employ deep learning tools in designing data-driven methods for solving PDEs (Han et al., 2018; Long et al., 2018; Sirignano & Spiliopoulos, 2018; Raissi et al., 2019). Most of these approaches are based on fully-connected neural networks (FCNNs), convolutional neural networks(CNNs) and multilayer perceptron (MLP). These neural network structures usually require an increment of the layers to improve the predictive accuracy (Raissi et al., 2019), and subsequently lead to a more complicated model due to the additional parameters. Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) are one type of neural network architectures. RNNs predict the next time step value by using the input data from the current

and previous states and share parameters across all inputs. This idea (Sherstinsky, 2020) of using current and previous step states to calculate the state at the next time step is not unique to RNNs. In fact, it is ubiquitously used in numerical PDEs. Almost all time-stepping numerical methods applied to solve time-dependent PDEs, such as Euler’s, Crank-Nicolson, high-order Taylor and its variance Runge-Kutta (Ascher et al., 1997) time-stepping methods, update numerical solution by utilizing solution from previous steps.

This motivates us to think what would happen if we replace the previous step data in the neural network with numerical solution data to PDE supported on grids. It is possible that the neural network behaves like a time-stepping method, for example, forward Euler’s method yields the numerical solution at a new time point as the current state output (Chen et al., 2018). Since the numerical solution on each of the grid point (for finite difference) or grid cell (for finite element) computed at a set of contiguous time points can be treated as neural network input in the form of one time sequence of data, the deep learning framework can be trained to predict any time-dependent PDEs from the time series data supported on some grids if the bidirectional structure is applied (Huang et al., 2015; Schuster & Paliwal, 1997). In other words, the supervised training process can be regarded as a practice of the deep learning framework to learn the numerical solution from the input data, by learning the coefficients on neural network layers.

Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997) is a neural network built upon RNNs. Unlike vanilla RNNs, which suffer from losing long term information and high probability of gradient vanishing or exploding, LSTM has a specifically designed memory cell with a set of new gates such as input gate and forget gate. Equipped with these new gates which control the time to preserve and pass the information, LSTM is capable of learning long term dependencies without the danger of having gradient vanishing or exploding. In the past two decades, LSTM has been widely used in the field of natural language processing (NLP), such as machine translation, dialogue systems, question answering systems (Lipton et al., 2015).

Inspired by numerical PDE schemes and LSTM neural network, we propose a new deep learning framework, denoted as Neural-PDE. It simulates multi-dimensional governing laws, represented by time-dependent PDEs, from time series data generated on some grids and predicts the next _n_ time steps data. The Neural-PDE is capable of intelligently processing related data from all spatial grids by using the bidirectional (Schuster & Paliwal, 1997) neural network, and thus guarantees the accuracy of the numerical solution and the feasibility in learning any time-dependent PDEs. The detailed structures of the Neural-PDE and data normalization are introduced in Section 3.

The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 briefly reviews finite difference method for solving PDEs. Section 3 contains detailed description of designing the Neural-PDE. In Section 4 and Appendix A of the paper, we apply the Neural-PDE to solve four different PDEs, including the 1-dimensional(1D) wave equation, the 2-dimensional(2D) heat equation, and two systems of PDEs: the invicid Burgers’ equations and a coupled Navier Stokes-Cahn Hilliard equations, which widely appear in multiscale modeling of complex fluid systems. We demonstrate the robustness of the Neural-PDE, which achieves convergence within 20 epochs with an admissible mean squared error, even when we add Gaussian noise in the input data.

Neural-PDE is able to outperform PINN while having much less parameters, where PINN contains 4 hidden layers with 200 neurons per layer and Neural-PDE only consists of 3 layers (2 bi-lstm with 20 neurons per layer and 1 dense output layer with 10 neurons).

## EXAMPLE: INVISCID BURGERS’ EQUATION
Inviscid Burgers’ equation is a classical nonlinear PDE in fluid dynamics. In this example, we consider an invicid Burgers’ equation which has the following hyperbolic form:

and with initial and boundary conditions:

The invicid Burgers’ equation is hard to deal with in numerical PDEs due to the discontinuities (shock waves) in the solutions. We use a upwinding finite difference scheme to create the training data and put the velocity _u, v_ in to the input matrix. Let _δx_ = _δy_ = 10 _[−]_[2] _, δt_ = 10 _[−]_[3] , our empirical results (see Figure 4) show that the Neural-PDE is able to learn the shock waves, boundary conditions and the rules of the equation, and predict _u_ and _v_ simultaneously with an overall MSE of 1 _._ 4018 _×_ 10 _[−]_[5] . The complex fluids system has the following features: the micro-structures such as the molecular configurations, the interaction between different scales and the competition between multi-phase fluids (Hyon et al., 2010). Another challenge of deep learning in solving a system like this is how to process the data to improve the learning efficiency when the input matrix consists of variables such as _φ ∈_ [0 _,_ 1] with large magnitude value and variable of very small values such as _p ∼_ 10 _[−]_[5] . Our Neural-PDE prediction shows that the physical features of _p_ and _φ_ have been successfully captured with an overall MSE: 6 _._ 1631 _×_ 10 _[−]_[7] (see Figure 7).

## 5 CONCLUSIONS

In this paper, we proposed a novel sequence recurrent deep learning framework: Neural-PDE, which is capable of intelligently filtering and learning solutions of time-dependent PDEs. One key innovation of our method is that the time marching method from the numerical PDEs is applied in the deep learning framework, and the neural network is trained to explore the accurate numerical solutions for prediction.

The state-of-the-art researches have shown the promising power of deep learning in solving highdimensional nonlinear problems in engineering, biology and finance with efficiency in computation and accuracy in prediction. However, there are still unresolved issues in applying deep learning in PDEs. For instance, the stability and convergence of the numerical algorithms have been rigorously studied by applied mathematicians. Due to the high nonlinearity of the neural network system and the curse of dimensionality (Hutzenthaler et al., 2019), theorems guiding stability and convergence of solutions predicted by the neural network are to be revealed.

Lastly, it would be helpful and interesting if one can theoretically characterize a numerical scheme from the neural network coefficients and learn the forms or mechanics from the scheme and prediction. We leave these questions for further study.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper proposes "Neural-PDE," a sequence-to-sequence framework using bidirectional LSTMs to learn and predict time-dependent PDE solutions from grid-based time series data. While the motivation—drawing an analogy between RNN time-stepping and classical numerical integrators (Euler, Runge-Kutta, etc.)—is intuitive and well-articulated, the paper suffers from several significant weaknesses in both novelty and rigor. First, the methodological contribution appears incremental: applying bidirectional LSTMs to PDE time series is a fairly direct application of well-established architectures, and the paper does not adequately situate itself against closely related prior work on neural PDE solvers (e.g., PDE-Net, Fourier Neural Operators, DeepONet, or other RNN/LSTM-based approaches for dynamical systems), making it difficult to assess what is genuinely new. Second, the claim that Neural-PDE "learns governing rules" without knowledge of the PDE form is overstated—the method learns a black-box grid-to-grid mapping, which is quite different from discovering governing equations, and the abstract/introduction blur this distinction. Third, the efficiency claim regarding parameter sharing versus CNN/MLP is asserted rather than quantitatively substantiated, and the comparison baselines are not yet clearly defined. On clarity, the writing is generally readable but contains grammatical issues ("invicid," "great quantity of parameters for model precision") and some imprecise statements (e.g., conflating RNN time-stepping with the convergence properties of numerical integrators without theoretical justification). Finally, the evaluation on only four relatively standard PDEs with a focus on "convergence within 20 epochs" and MSE does not provide a rigorous benchmark—generalization across initial/boundary conditions, stability over long rollouts, comparison with established neural PDE baselines, and scaling behavior would be essential to support the broad claims made. Overall, the paper presents a reasonable engineering idea but needs substantially stronger positioning, more careful claims, and a more rigorous experimental protocol to meet the bar of a top-tier venue.

comments.title: Finite difference grid spacing formula has wrong sign
comments.explanation: The grid spacing should be δx = (x_b - x_a)/N_x (a positive quantity when x_b > x_a), but the formula as written appears to be (x_a - x_b)/N_x, which gives a negative spacing. Even allowing for OCR issues with fraction rendering, the ordering of x_a and x_b in the numerator is reversed from the standard convention. Given that this is a review of finite differences and the sign of δx matters for subsequent difference formulas, this is a real error (assuming the OCR preserved the order correctly, which it appears to have since the same reversed pattern appears for both δx and δy).

comments.title: Claim that 'u is known' contradicts the purpose of solving the PDE
comments.quote: where _u_ = _u_ ( _t, x_ 1 _, ..., xn_ ) is known, _xi ∈_ R are spatial variables, and the operator _f_ maps R _→_ R.
comments.explanation: Saying u is 'known' is confusing and contradictory — the whole point of a PDE is that u is unknown and must be solved for. The authors likely meant 'u is the unknown function' or 'u is the function of interest.' This is a minor framing issue in the preliminaries that any careful reader would infer correctly, but it is technically wrong as stated.

comments.title: Operator f codomain specification is inconsistent with usage
comments.quote: the operator _f_ maps R _→_ R
comments.explanation: The text says f maps R to R, but in the subsequent example f is 'the Laplacian operator Δ,' which is a differential operator acting on functions, not a map from R to R. Moreover, in equation (2.2.1), f takes multiple arguments (x, y, u_x, u_y, u_xx, u_yy), so its domain is clearly not R. The signature R→R is too simplistic to describe the range of PDE operators the paper considers.

comments.title: Undefined symbol ν in upwinding scheme
comments.explanation: The function f_2 for the upwinding scheme uses a coefficient ν that has not been introduced or defined in the passage. Presumably this is the wave/advection speed from the 1D equation u_t + ν u_x = 0, but since the actual equation is not shown in the readable text, the reader has to infer ν's meaning. This is a minor specification gap.

comments.title: Claim that network 'regresses δx, δt coefficients as constants' is vague
comments.quote: if we map all our grid data into an input matrix which contains the information of _δx, δt_ , then the neural network would regress such coefficients as constants and will learn and filter the physical rules from all the _k_ mesh grids data
comments.explanation: This is a key conceptual claim about how the Neural-PDE works — that it implicitly learns grid spacings as constants. However, the input matrix described in Section 3.3 is just the solution values on grid points at discrete times; there is no explicit encoding of δx or δt in the input. The claim that the network can recover these grid parameters is asserted without justification or experimental verification. A careful reader would want to know: how does the network 'know' δx given only values? The statement is plausible but is presented as obvious when it is not.

comments.title: Dimension of grid points described as K
comments.quote: we map the PDE solution data onto a _K × N_ matrix, where _K ∈_ Z[+] is the dimension of the grid points
comments.explanation: Initially I thought 'dimension of the grid points' was a loose phrasing for 'number of grid points'. Checking the summary and Section 3.1, the authors use k for the number of collocation points (e.g., {a^N_1, ..., a^N_k}). Here K is clearly meant to be the total number of grid points (K = N_x × N_y per Figure 2's caption), not a 'dimension'. This is a minor terminology issue that could momentarily mislead a reader into thinking K refers to spatial dimensionality.

comments.title: Claim of outperforming PINN lacks supporting detail
comments.quote: Neural-PDE is able to outperform PINN while having much less parameters, where PINN contains 4 hidden layers with 200 neurons per layer and Neural-PDE only consists of 3 layers (2 bi-lstm with 20 neurons per layer and 1 dense output layer with 10 neurons).
comments.explanation: The comparison is stated but Table 2 contents are not shown in the passage. More importantly, PINN is typically evaluated as an unsupervised/physics-constrained method that does not require simulation training data, whereas Neural-PDE is trained supervised on simulated data. Comparing test MSE directly without addressing this fundamentally different problem setting (data-driven vs. physics-informed with known PDE form) risks overstating the comparison. The paper's own claim is that Neural-PDE learns without knowing the PDE — but PINN is typically *given* the PDE, so the comparison axis is unclear.

comments.title: Sigmoid normalization of pressure is underspecified
comments.quote: we normalized the _p_ data with a _sigmoid_ function
comments.explanation: The authors describe normalizing p with 'a sigmoid function' but do not specify parameters (scaling, centering) nor how the prediction is inverted back to physical p values for the reported MSE of 6.1631×10^{-7}. Since p ~ 10^{-5}, naively applying sigmoid(p) would map all values to ≈0.5 with negligible variation, so some scaling must be implied. It is also unclear whether the reported MSE is on normalized or original-scale p. This matters because the headline MSE number for the Cahn–Hilliard–Navier–Stokes example depends on this choice, and a reader cannot reproduce the result as stated.

comments.title: Training/test split description conflicts with prediction protocol
comments.quote: the whole dataset is randomly splitted in 80% for traning and 20% for testing. We will predict the next _tp ∈_ [31 _× δt,_ 40 _× δt_ ] PDE solution by using its previous _ttr ∈_ [0 _,_ 30 _× δt_ ] data
comments.explanation: The first statement describes a random 80/20 split; the second describes predicting the last 10 time steps (25%) from the first 30 (75%). These are two different partitioning schemes. One possibility is that the 80/20 split refers to splitting across different initial-condition trajectories while the time window split is within each trajectory, but this is not explained. As written it is ambiguous how the data were partitioned, which affects interpretation of the reported test MSEs.

comments.title: Missing equation content in passage (data-initialization matrix form)
comments.quote: the input and output data for _t ∈_ (0 _, T_ ) will be of the form:
comments.explanation: The prose introduces 'the input and output data ... will be of the form:' but no equation follows in the passage. Similarly 'it will automatically extract the information from the time series data as:' is followed by no displayed equation. I first considered that OCR might have dropped the equations, which is plausible. However, as extracted, the passage leaves the reader without the formal definition being promised — the K×N matrix and the bidirectional concatenation operation are referenced but not shown. If these are in fact missing, the data-formatting procedure is underspecified in Section 3.3.

comments.title: Claim that shock waves are 'learned' is an overstatement for smooth example
comments.quote: the Neural-PDE is able to learn the shock waves, boundary conditions and the rules of the equation
comments.explanation: The inviscid Burgers' IC/BC are not shown in the passage but the predicted solutions in Figure 4 appear smooth with peak magnitudes ≈0.3–0.4 (u) and ≈0.15–0.25 (v). With δt = 10^{-3} predicting to t=1, a genuine shock may or may not have formed depending on initial data. Claiming the network 'learned the shock waves' without demonstrating a discontinuity in the test solution overstates the result. A reader could mistakenly believe the method handles genuine shock discontinuities, which is a much stronger (and empirically unsupported here) claim.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and timely problem—data-driven prediction of time-dependent PDE dynamics—and the idea of using a Seq2Seq/BiLSTM architecture as a learned time-stepping surrogate is potentially interesting. However, based on the opening sections, the paper currently overstates its contributions and lacks the precision expected for an ICLR submission. Claims such as learning “any time-dependent PDE,” “guaranteeing” accuracy, and learning initial/boundary conditions and differential operators without PDE knowledge are not adequately justified and should be significantly qualified. The motivation draws broad analogies between RNN recurrence and numerical time integration, but it does not clearly articulate what is novel relative to existing neural PDE solvers, neural operators, ConvLSTM-based spatiotemporal models, PINNs, PDE-Net, or sequence models for dynamical systems. The technical description is also vague: it is unclear how spatial structure is represented, how boundary conditions are encoded, whether the model generalizes across grids, resolutions, parameters, or initial conditions, and how stability/error accumulation over long rollouts is handled. The writing contains numerous grammatical issues, imprecise terminology, and occasional mathematical inaccuracies or formatting problems, which reduce clarity and credibility. The most significant issues to address are: sharpening the contribution relative to prior work, replacing sweeping claims with rigorous empirical/theoretical support, clarifying the architecture and data assumptions, and providing stronger baselines and evaluation protocols that demonstrate genuine PDE learning rather than interpolation or short-term sequence prediction on fixed simulated datasets.

comments.title: Unsupported claims that the BiLSTM architecture guarantees accuracy, stability, or applicability to arbitrary time-dependent PDEs
comments.quote: The Neural-PDE is capable of intelligently processing related data from all spatial grids by using the bidirectional (Schuster & Paliwal, 1997) neural network, and thus guarantees the accuracy of the numerical solution and the feasibility in learning any time-dependent PDEs.
comments.explanation: The bidirectional recurrent architecture may help propagate information along a chosen flattened grid sequence, but it does not by itself imply learnability, consistency, stability, convergence, long-time accuracy, or applicability to arbitrary time-dependent PDEs. Similar overstatements appear where the paper suggests that BiLSTMs make the method trainable for “any” time-dependent PDE and where LSTM mitigation of vanishing gradients is treated as improving the “stability of the numerical approach.” Training stability and numerical PDE stability are different concepts. The experiments cover selected benchmark systems and do not establish guarantees for stiff, chaotic, discontinuous, multiscale, high-dimensional, or out-of-distribution PDEs.

comments.title: Prediction accuracy is overstated as learning operators, coefficients, boundary/initial conditions, and physical rules
comments.quote: The results show that the Neural-PDE is capable of learning the initial conditions, boundary conditions and differential operators defining the initial-boundary-value problem of a PDE system without the knowledge of the specific form of the PDE system.
comments.explanation: The paper demonstrates supervised prediction of solver-generated solution trajectories, but that is not the same as identifying the differential operator, boundary conditions, initial conditions, coefficients, or governing physical rules. Many PDEs or boundary conditions can agree on a finite set of observed trajectories, especially on a fixed grid and under fixed initial/boundary data. Later statements that the model learns coefficients, physical rules, shock waves, or boundary conditions from low MSE and visual agreement have the same problem: they show accurate reproduction of tested data, not validated PDE discovery or transferable rule learning.

comments.title: Parameter-sharing comparison with CNNs/MLPs overstates the computational advantage
comments.quote: Furthermore, unlike the traditional machine learning approaches for learning PDEs, such as CNN and MLP, which require great quantity of parameters for model precision, the Neural-PDE shares parameters among all time steps, and thus considerably reduces computational complexity and leads to a fast learning algorithm.
comments.explanation: Recurrent models do share weights across sequence steps, which can reduce parameter count relative to some architectures. However, the comparison is too broad. CNNs also use parameter sharing, MLP/PINN parameter counts depend heavily on architecture and training setup, and computational complexity is not determined only by parameter count. BiLSTMs require sequential recurrent computation over the chosen sequence dimension and two directional passes. The paper’s specific parameter comparison does not justify the general claim that CNN/MLP methods inherently require many parameters or that Neural-PDE generally reduces computational complexity.

comments.title: Flattening grid data into a sequence leaves spatial geometry and ordering dependence unresolved
comments.quote: There is no regularization for the input order of the grid points data in the matrix because of the bi-directional structure of the Neural-PDE.
comments.explanation: A BiLSTM is still order-sensitive: changing the ordering of grid points changes the sequence processed by the recurrent layers. Bidirectionality gives access to past and future positions in the chosen sequence, but it does not make the model permutation-invariant, encode mesh connectivity, preserve multidimensional locality, or represent metric distances. This also affects claims that the method learns multidimensional governing laws from grid time series, because the paper does not clearly specify how coordinates, grid spacing, boundary locations, mesh connectivity, or flattening order are represented.

comments.title: The input matrix is said to contain grid and time spacings, but the representation appears to contain only solution values
comments.quote: For a time-dependent PDE, if we map all our grid data into an input matrix which contains the information of _δx, δt_ , then the neural network would regress such coefficients as constants and will learn and filter the physical rules from all the _k_ mesh grids data as:
comments.explanation: The surrounding description defines each row of the input matrix as a time series of solution values at a grid point, and later notation lists values such as _u_i^0, …, u_i^N_ rather than explicit _δx_ or _δt_ features. If all data use a fixed spatial and temporal discretization, the network may implicitly absorb those constants, but that is different from the input matrix containing spacing information. This matters for coefficient learning and transfer across different discretizations.

comments.title: The PDE definition contains misleading notation for the unknown solution and operator type
comments.quote: where _u_ = _u_ ( _t, x_ 1 _, ..., xn_ ) is known, _xi ∈_ R are spatial variables, and the operator _f_ maps R _→_ R. For example, consider the parabolic heat equation: _ut_ = _α_[2] ∆ _u_ , where _u_ represents the temperature and _f_ is the Laplacian operator ∆.
comments.explanation: In a PDE definition, the dependent variable _u_ is normally the unknown solution, not a known quantity. The statement that _f_ maps R→R is also misleading for a PDE operator involving spatial derivatives; the Laplacian acts on functions, and the later multidimensional formulation has arguments such as _x, y, u_x, u_y, u_{xx}, u_{yy}_. In the heat-equation example, the right-hand-side operator is more accurately _α²∆_ unless the coefficient is being treated separately. The intended meaning is recoverable, but the notation is mathematically imprecise.

comments.title: Grid definitions are inconsistent with the stated spatial domain
comments.quote: where _tn_ = _nδt,_ 0 _≤ n ≤ N_ , and _δt_ = _N[T]_[for] _[t][ ∈]_[[0] _[, T]_[]][, and some large integer] _[ N]_[.] _[x][i]_[=] _[ iδx,]_[0] _[ ≤] i ≤ Nx_ , _δx_ = _[x][a] N[−] x[x][b]_ for _x ∈_ [ _xa, xb_ ]. _yj_ = _jδy,_ 0 _≤ j ≤ Ny_ , _δy_ = _[y][a] N[−] y[y][b]_ for _y ∈_ [ _ya, yb_ ].
comments.explanation: Some of the displayed formulas may be OCR-corrupted, but as written they are inconsistent with the domain Ω = [_x_a_, _x_b_] × [_y_a_, _y_b_]. One would normally define _x_i = x_a + iδx_ with _δx = (x_b - x_a)/N_x_, and similarly for _y_j_. The passage instead uses _x_i = iδx_ and _y_j = jδy_, which only fits domains starting at zero, and the apparent spacing formulas have sign or quotient errors. Because these definitions underlie the finite-difference stencil, they should be corrected explicitly.

comments.title: The finite-difference stencil is described as using four points, but the update also depends on the center point
comments.quote: The scheme (2.2.7) updates _u[n] i,j_[+1] using four points of _u[n]_ values (see Figure 1).
comments.explanation: For a two-dimensional central-difference stencil involving _u_x, u_y, u_{xx}, u_{yy}_ and an explicit update of _u_{i,j}^{n+1}_, the center value _u_{i,j}^n_ is also part of the update. The usual description would be a five-point stencil, or “the four neighboring points plus the center point.” The current wording could mislead readers about the actual stencil.

comments.title: Experimental splitting and sequence-window construction are underspecified and may allow leakage
comments.quote: In the following examples, the whole dataset is randomly splitted in 80% for traning and 20% for testing.
comments.explanation: For PDE trajectory data, the unit of the random split is crucial. Randomly splitting grid-point time series, time windows, or space-time samples can leak information through neighboring grid points or adjacent times and produce overly optimistic test MSEs. The paper also does not clearly distinguish the input time window from the supervised target window, and the prediction horizon is ambiguous relative to later figures at much later physical times. It is unclear whether evaluation uses sliding windows, recursive rollout, held-out trajectories, held-out time intervals, or another protocol. Related notation also uses _N_ both as a final time index and as a number of samples, making exact tensor shapes harder to reproduce.

comments.title: Comparison with PINN is not sufficiently controlled
comments.quote: Table 2 shows the comparison results of our proposed Neural-PDE with the state-of-the-art method Physically Informed Artificial Neural Networks (PINN) (Raissi et al., 2019) on two PDEs (1 _d_ Allen-Cahn and 1 _d_ Burgers’ equation). Neural-PDE is able to outperform PINN while having much less parameters, where PINN contains 4 hidden layers with 200 neurons per layer and Neural-PDE only consists of 3 layers (2 bi-lstm with 20 neurons per layer and 1 dense output layer with 10 neurons).
comments.explanation: Neural-PDE and PINN typically address different learning setups: PINNs use PDE residuals and sparse/boundary data, whereas the proposed method appears to train on dense numerical solution trajectories. The passage does not specify whether both methods receive comparable data, use the same train/test points, are evaluated on the same domains and variables, or receive comparable optimization budgets. Without these details, the claim that Neural-PDE “outperforms” PINN may reflect different data access or task formulation rather than a controlled architectural advantage.

comments.title: Pressure normalization by sigmoid is underspecified and potentially problematic
comments.quote: For the Neural-PDE to better extract and learn the physical features of variables in different scales, we normalized the _p_ data with a _sigmoid_ function.
comments.explanation: The paper correctly recognizes that variables at different scales may need normalization, but the actual transformation is unclear. Applying a raw sigmoid to pressure values around 10^-5 would map them all close to 0.5 and may compress meaningful variation unless an affine scaling is applied first. The paper also does not state whether MSE is computed before or after inverse normalization, or how the transformation handles pressure sign and the arbitrary additive constant common in incompressible-flow pressure.

comments.title: Numerical solver output is described as an exact solution
comments.quote: We use a upwinding finite difference scheme to create the training data
comments.explanation: Using a finite-difference solver as reference data is appropriate for supervised experiments, but the resulting data should not be called the “exact solution” unless an analytic solution is actually used. It is more accurately a numerical reference solution or ground-truth dataset. The distinction matters for inviscid Burgers’ equations because shocks and numerical diffusion make the solver choice part of the target being learned.

comments.title: The absence of stability and convergence theory is stated too broadly
comments.quote: Due to the high nonlinearity of the neural network system and the curse of dimensionality (Hutzenthaler et al., 2019), theorems guiding stability and convergence of solutions predicted by the neural network are to be revealed.
comments.explanation: The paper is right that this particular BiLSTM-based time-advancement method lacks classical PDE-solver stability, convergence, and long-time error theory. However, the statement is too sweeping if read as saying such theory is generally absent for neural PDE methods. There are theoretical results for selected neural-network PDE solvers, approximation rates, PINNs, Deep Ritz-type methods, neural operators, and semilinear parabolic PDE approximations. The unresolved gap should be framed more specifically as the lack of stability/convergence theory for this proposed data-driven Neural-PDE framework.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and timely problem—data-driven prediction of time-dependent PDE dynamics—and the idea of using a Seq2Seq/BiLSTM architecture as a learned time-stepping surrogate is potentially interesting. However, based on the opening sections, the paper currently overstates its contributions and lacks the precision expected for an ICLR submission. Claims such as learning “any time-dependent PDE,” “guaranteeing” accuracy, and learning initial/boundary conditions and differential operators without PDE knowledge are not adequately justified and should be significantly qualified. The motivation draws broad analogies between RNN recurrence and numerical time integration, but it does not clearly articulate what is novel relative to existing neural PDE solvers, neural operators, ConvLSTM-based spatiotemporal models, PINNs, PDE-Net, or sequence models for dynamical systems. The technical description is also vague: it is unclear how spatial structure is represented, how boundary conditions are encoded, whether the model generalizes across grids, resolutions, parameters, or initial conditions, and how stability/error accumulation over long rollouts is handled. The writing contains numerous grammatical issues, imprecise terminology, and occasional mathematical inaccuracies or formatting problems, which reduce clarity and credibility. The most significant issues to address are: sharpening the contribution relative to prior work, replacing sweeping claims with rigorous empirical/theoretical support, clarifying the architecture and data assumptions, and providing stronger baselines and evaluation protocols that demonstrate genuine PDE learning rather than interpolation or short-term sequence prediction on fixed simulated datasets.

comments.title: Overstated claim that bidirectional LSTM makes the method applicable to any time-dependent PDE
comments.quote: Since the numerical solution on each of the grid point (for finite difference) or grid cell (for finite element) computed at a set of contiguous time points can be treated as neural network input in the form of one time sequence of data, the deep learning framework can be trained to predict any time-dependent PDEs from the time series data supported on some grids if the bidirectional structure is applied (Huang et al., 2015; Schuster & Paliwal, 1997).
comments.explanation: I understand the intended analogy: numerical PDE solvers use local stencil information from previous time levels, and a bidirectional recurrent architecture may help propagate information across an ordered representation of grid points. Later sections do describe reshaping grid data into a sequence and using BiLSTMs. However, the phrase “any time-dependent PDEs” is much stronger than what the paper justifies. A bidirectional architecture by itself does not guarantee learnability, stability, consistency, or long-time predictive accuracy for arbitrary PDEs, especially for stiff, chaotic, discontinuous, multiscale, or parameter-varying systems. Standard numerical analysis would require assumptions on regularity, discretization, stability/CFL-type constraints, and approximation error; none of those are supplied here. The experiments later cover only a small set of benchmark systems, so the universal claim remains unsupported.

comments.title: Claim of learning differential operators and boundary/initial conditions is stronger than demonstrated
comments.quote: The results show that the Neural-PDE is capable of learning the initial conditions, boundary conditions and differential operators defining the initial-boundary-value problem of a PDE system without the knowledge of the specific form of the PDE system.
comments.explanation: The paper’s goal of learning solution evolution from data is clear, and the later examples do show low prediction MSE on held-out portions of numerically generated trajectories. But prediction of a solution trajectory is not the same as identifying the initial condition, boundary condition, or differential operator. Many different PDEs or boundary conditions can agree on a finite set of observed trajectories, so operator identification is generally non-identifiable without additional structure, probing data, or explicit regression of the operator. From the context, the model appears to learn a data-driven solution map for a fixed discretized problem, not an interpretable or separately validated differential operator/BC/IC. This wording could mislead readers into thinking the method performs PDE discovery in the sense of recovering governing equations.

comments.title: Use of “guarantees” is not supported by theory or experiments
comments.quote: The Neural-PDE is capable of intelligently processing related data from all spatial grids by using the bidirectional (Schuster & Paliwal, 1997) neural network, and thus guarantees the accuracy of the numerical solution and the feasibility in learning any time-dependent PDEs.
comments.explanation: The authors reasonably motivate bidirectional recurrence as a way to pass information along a flattened spatial grid representation. I checked the later methodological sections and experiments to see whether a convergence, stability, or approximation theorem is provided, but the support appears empirical. In numerical PDEs, “guarantees the accuracy” has a strong meaning, usually involving consistency, stability, convergence, or error bounds. A BiLSTM architecture does not by itself provide such guarantees, and flattening multidimensional grid data into a sequence can make locality/order dependence nontrivial. The experiments may show good accuracy on selected data sets, but they do not establish guaranteed accuracy or feasibility for arbitrary time-dependent PDEs.

comments.title: Parameter-sharing comparison with CNNs/MLPs overstates computational advantage
comments.quote: Furthermore, unlike the traditional machine learning approaches for learning PDEs, such as CNN and MLP, which require great quantity of parameters for model precision, the Neural-PDE shares parameters among all time steps, and thus considerably reduces computational complexity and leads to a fast learning algorithm.
comments.explanation: The authors are right that recurrent models share weights across sequence steps, which can reduce parameter count relative to some unrolled or fully connected sequence models. However, the comparison is too broad as written. CNNs also share parameters spatially, and PINN/MLP parameter counts depend heavily on architecture, resolution treatment, and training setup. Computational complexity is also not determined solely by parameter count: BiLSTMs require sequential recurrent computation over the chosen sequence dimension and, in bidirectional form, two passes; training cost scales with sequence length and hidden size. Later tables compare against a particular PINN architecture, but that does not justify the general claim that CNN/MLP methods inherently require many parameters or that Neural-PDE “considerably reduces computational complexity” in general.

comments.title: Method claim does not specify how spatial geometry and grid spacing are represented
comments.quote: It simulates multi-dimensional governing laws, represented by time-dependent PDEs, from time series data generated on some grids and predicts the next _n_ time steps data.
comments.explanation: At a high level this is an acceptable overview sentence, and Section 3 later explains that PDE solution data are reshaped into a matrix over grid points and time. Still, the claim that the model learns multidimensional governing laws from grid time series raises an important reproducibility and identifiability question: how are spatial coordinates, mesh connectivity, grid spacing, boundary locations, and variable ordering encoded? These quantities are essential for PDE dynamics. The later context suggests that the authors flatten multidimensional grids into a 1D sequence and rely on bidirectionality, but it is not clear that this preserves neighborhood structure or distinguishes two data sets with identical value sequences but different grid spacings/geometries. This does not invalidate the experiments on fixed grids, but it makes the broader “governing laws” claim under-specified.

comments.title: Solution variable is described as known in the PDE definition
comments.quote: where _u_ = _u_ ( _t, x_ 1 _, ..., xn_ ) is known, _xi ∈_ R are spatial variables, and the operator _f_ maps R _→_ R.
comments.explanation: I initially considered whether “known” might mean “given as a function after numerical solution” or might be an OCR corruption of “unknown.” In the context of defining a time-dependent PDE, however, the dependent variable _u_ is normally the unknown solution to be solved for, while the independent variables and the operator are specified. Later parts of the paper also discuss predicting or learning the solution, which reinforces that _u_ should not be treated as known at the PDE-definition stage. This is likely a simple wording error, but as written it reverses the standard mathematical role of the solution variable.

comments.title: The mapping type of the PDE operator is too narrow and inconsistent with the examples
comments.quote: the operator _f_ maps R _→_ R. For example, consider the parabolic heat equation: _ut_ = _α_[2] ∆ _u_ , where _u_ represents the temperature and _f_ is the Laplacian operator ∆.
comments.explanation: I tried to read the mapping statement leniently as an informal way of saying that the right-hand side is scalar-valued. But for a PDE, especially one involving spatial derivatives, _f_ is not simply a map from R to R in the ordinary scalar-function sense. In the later two-dimensional model, _f_ is written as depending on _x, y, u_x, u_y, u_{xx}, u_{yy}_, so its pointwise arguments would be multidimensional; for the heat equation, the Laplacian is a differential operator acting on functions, not a scalar map R→R. The intended meaning is recoverable, but the stated type of _f_ is mathematically misleading.

comments.title: Heat-equation operator omits the diffusivity factor in the prose
comments.quote: consider the parabolic heat equation: _ut_ = _α_[2] ∆ _u_ , where _u_ represents the temperature and _f_ is the Laplacian operator ∆.
comments.explanation: The example is standard except for a small inconsistency in identifying the right-hand-side operator. If the PDE is _u_t = α^2 Δu_, then the operator appearing on the right-hand side is really _f = α^2 Δ_ unless the authors are implicitly setting _α = 1_ or treating _α^2_ as a separate coefficient outside _f_. This is not a serious mathematical obstacle, because the intended heat equation is clear, but the wording is slightly inaccurate.

comments.title: Grid definitions appear inconsistent with the stated spatial domain
comments.quote: where _tn_ = _nδt,_ 0 _≤ n ≤ N_ , and _δt_ = _N[T]_[for] _[t][ ∈]_[[0] _[, T]_[]][, and some large integer] _[ N]_[.] _[x][i]_[=] _[ iδx,]_[0] _[ ≤] i ≤ Nx_ , _δx_ = _[x][a] N[−] x[x][b]_ for _x ∈_ [ _xa, xb_ ]. _yj_ = _jδy,_ 0 _≤ j ≤ Ny_ , _δy_ = _[y][a] N[−] y[y][b]_ for _y ∈_ [ _ya, yb_ ].
comments.explanation: Some of this is plausibly OCR-corrupted: for example, _δt_ was probably intended to be _T/N_, and the spatial spacings were probably intended to be quotients involving _x_b-x_a_ and _y_b-y_a_. Even granting the most plausible corrections, the displayed definitions as written are inconsistent with the domain Ω = [_x_a_, _x_b_] × [_y_a_, _y_b_]. One would normally define _x_i = x_a + iδx_ with _δx = (x_b-x_a)/N_x_, and similarly for _y_j_. The passage instead has _x_i=iδx_ and _y_j=jδy_, which only fits domains beginning at zero, and the apparent signs of the spacings are reversed. Because these definitions underlie the finite-difference stencil, the notation should be corrected explicitly.

comments.title: The finite-difference stencil is described as using four points, but the update also depends on the center point
comments.quote: The scheme (2.2.7) updates _u[n] i,j_[+1] using four points of _u[n]_ values (see Figure 1).
comments.explanation: I understand the authors' intent: for a two-dimensional central-difference stencil, information is taken from the four neighboring grid points. However, for a PDE depending on _u_x, u_y, u_{xx}, u_{yy}_ and an explicit update of _u_{i,j}^{n+1}_, the value _u_{i,j}^n_ itself is also part of the update. Figure 1 appears to show the center point together with its four neighbors. Thus the usual description would be a five-point stencil, or “the four neighboring points plus the center point.” The current wording could mislead a reader about the actual stencil used.

comments.title: Bidirectional LSTM does not by itself preserve arbitrary spatial neighborhood structure
comments.quote: In particular, we use the bidirectional LSTM (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997; Graves & Schmidhuber, 2005) to better retain the state information from data on grid points which are neighbourhoods in the mesh but far away in input matrix.
comments.explanation: The motivation is understandable: after flattening a spatial grid into a sequence, a bidirectional recurrent model can propagate information from both directions in that sequence. What remains problematic is the implied connection between this mechanism and mesh-neighborhood structure. A BiLSTM has an ordering bias along the chosen one-dimensional sequence; it does not inherently know that two entries far apart in the flattened order are adjacent in the original two-dimensional or unstructured mesh, nor does it encode metric distances or stencil connectivity unless those are supplied or enforced. Later statements that the bidirectional structure can process all grid data do not resolve this, because access to sequence context is not the same as respecting PDE locality. This is a methodological gap rather than merely a wording issue.

comments.title: The input matrix is said to contain δx and δt, but the described data representation appears to contain only solution values
comments.quote: For a time-dependent PDE, if we map all our grid data into an input matrix which contains the information of _δx, δt_ , then the neural network would regress such coefficients as constants and will learn and filter the physical rules from all the _k_ mesh grids data as:
comments.explanation: I first checked whether δx and δt might be included later as explicit features. In the surrounding description, however, each row of the input matrix is a time series of solution values at a grid point, and the later notation for _a_i^N_ likewise lists only values such as _u_i^0, …, u_i^N_. If all training data use a fixed grid spacing and fixed time step, then the network may implicitly absorb those constants, but that is different from the input matrix containing δx and δt. This matters because the claim about learning coefficients or physical rules would not automatically transfer across different discretizations unless spacing information is represented explicitly or the training regime varies it.

comments.title: Learning physical rules and coefficients from flattened solution data is overstated
comments.quote: then the neural network would regress such coefficients as constants and will learn and filter the physical rules from all the _k_ mesh grids data
comments.explanation: The authors are right that a sufficiently expressive sequence model can learn a predictive map from past solution snapshots to future snapshots. But the statement goes further, suggesting that the model will regress coefficients and learn the physical rules. From solution trajectories alone, especially on a fixed grid with fixed boundary and initial conditions, the governing operator or coefficients are not generally identifiable without additional assumptions, varied data, or a model-discovery objective. The experiments may demonstrate accurate interpolation or short-term forecasting, but this sentence reads as a stronger system-identification claim than the method description justifies.

comments.title: Gradient-flow benefits of LSTMs are conflated with numerical stability
comments.quote: The LSTM neural network is designed to overcome the vanishing gradient issue through hidden layers, therefore we use such recurrent structure to increase the stability of the numerical approach in deep learning.
comments.explanation: It is fair to say that LSTMs were designed to mitigate vanishing-gradient problems in sequence training. However, numerical stability for PDE time stepping is a different concept, usually involving growth bounds, CFL-type restrictions, consistency/stability/convergence relationships, or long-time error behavior. The fact that LSTMs help gradient propagation during optimization does not by itself imply stability of the learned PDE simulator. The authors may intend “training stability,” but the phrase “stability of the numerical approach” could be read as a claim about PDE-solver stability, which is not established here.

comments.title: The supervised training targets are not clearly distinguished from the input time window
comments.quote: The data from time point 0 to _N_ are the training data set.
comments.explanation: The surrounding notation correctly defines past states _a_i^N_ as inputs and future states _b_i^M_ as predictions. But in supervised sequence-to-sequence training, the model also needs target values from times _N+1_ through _N+M_ to compute the MSE loss. Saying that only the data from time point 0 to _N_ are the training data set blurs the distinction between the input window and the labeled training examples. This may just be imprecise phrasing, but a reader trying to reproduce the method would need a clearer description of how input-output windows are constructed and split.

comments.title: Bidirectional LSTM does not remove dependence on grid-point ordering
comments.quote: There is no regularization for the input order of the grid points data in the matrix because of the bi-directional structure of the Neural-PDE.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended point: because a BiLSTM passes information in both directions along the flattened sequence, the authors hope it can connect grid points that are spatial neighbors but separated in one traversal direction. However, this does not make the model invariant or even robust to arbitrary permutations of grid points. A BiLSTM is still fundamentally order-sensitive: changing the order of the rows changes the sequence seen by the recurrent layers and therefore changes which points are treated as adjacent in hidden-state propagation. In a flattened 2D grid, the ordering convention is a substantive modeling choice, and arbitrary ordering can destroy locality. The concern is not resolved by the bidirectional structure; bidirectionality only gives access to past and future positions in the chosen sequence, not to unordered mesh adjacency. The paper should specify the flattening order, justify it, or use/describe a permutation- or graph-aware mechanism.

comments.title: Train/test split is underspecified and may allow spatial-temporal leakage
comments.quote: In the following examples, the whole dataset is randomly splitted in 80% for traning and 20% for testing.
comments.explanation: The authors are clearly trying to report a standard held-out test error, which is appropriate. But for PDE trajectories, the unit of random splitting matters a great deal. If individual grid-point time series, time windows, or space-time samples are randomly split, the test set may be highly correlated with the training set through neighboring grid points or adjacent times, producing overly optimistic MSEs. If instead entire trajectories, initial conditions, spatial subdomains, or time intervals are held out, that evaluates a different and stronger form of generalization. The context does not resolve this because the model input is described as using all collocation-point histories, yet the experiments say the whole dataset is randomly split. Without specifying the split unit and preventing leakage across nearby time/space samples, the reported test MSEs are hard to interpret.

comments.title: Prediction horizon is ambiguous relative to figures at later physical times
comments.quote: We will predict the next _tp ∈_ [31 _× δt,_ 40 _× δt_ ] PDE solution by using its previous _ttr ∈_ [0 _,_ 30 _× δt_ ] data as:
comments.explanation: The intended setup seems to be a sequence-to-sequence forecast from 31 previous time samples to the next 10 samples, which is reasonable. However, in the Burgers example the paper later sets \(\delta t=10^{-3}\), so the quoted interval corresponds only to predictions through \(t=0.04\) if interpreted literally from the initial time. The figures show predictions at time 1.0, so a careful reader needs to know whether the model is evaluated on sliding windows throughout the trajectory, recursively rolled out many times, or trained separately on shifted windows. These are very different prediction tasks: direct short-window interpolation near \(t=1\) is much easier than stable long-time rollout from \(t=0\). The text hints at “different permutations of training and predicting time periods,” but the exact evaluation protocol remains underspecified.

comments.title: Comparison with PINN is not sufficiently controlled
comments.quote: Table 2 shows the comparison results of our proposed Neural-PDE with the state-of-the-art method Physically Informed Artificial Neural Networks (PINN) (Raissi et al., 2019) on two PDEs (1 _d_ Allen-Cahn and 1 _d_ Burgers’ equation). Neural-PDE is able to outperform PINN while having much less parameters, where PINN contains 4 hidden layers with 200 neurons per layer and Neural-PDE only consists of 3 layers (2 bi-lstm with 20 neurons per layer and 1 dense output layer with 10 neurons).
comments.explanation: It is useful to compare against a known baseline such as PINN, and parameter count is a relevant practical consideration. The difficulty is that Neural-PDE and PINN typically solve different learning problems: PINNs use the PDE residual and sparse/boundary data, whereas this method appears to train on dense numerical solution trajectories generated by a solver. The passage does not state whether both methods receive comparable data, use the same train/test points, are evaluated on the same physical variables and domains, or receive comparable optimization budgets. Without those details, the claim that Neural-PDE “outperforms” PINN may conflate architecture quality with access to more supervised trajectory data or a different task formulation. This affects one of the paper’s headline empirical comparisons.

comments.title: Time-series matrix dimensions use \(N\) inconsistently
comments.quote: In order to feed data into our sequence model framework, we map the PDE solution data onto a _K × N_ matrix, where _K ∈_ Z[+] is the dimension of the grid points and _N ∈_ Z[+] is the length of the time series data on each grid point.
comments.explanation: The intended data layout is understandable: rows correspond to grid/collocation points and columns to time samples. However, earlier notation defines histories such as \(\mathbf a_i^N=[\hat u_i^0,\hat u_i^1,\ldots,\hat u_i^N]\), which contains \(N+1\) samples if time indices run from 0 to \(N\). The experimental description similarly uses data from \([0,30\delta t]\), which would include 31 sampled states under the usual convention. Thus \(N\) is sometimes an index of the last time point and sometimes the number of time samples. This is a localized notation issue, but it matters for reproducing the exact input/output tensor shapes.

comments.title: Pressure normalization by sigmoid is underspecified and potentially problematic
comments.quote: For the Neural-PDE to better extract and learn the physical features of variables in different scales, we normalized the _p_ data with a _sigmoid_ function.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly recognize a real multi-scale learning issue: variables such as \(\phi\) and \(p\) can have very different magnitudes, and normalization can strongly affect training. What remains unclear is the actual transformation. Applying a raw sigmoid to values \(p\sim 10^{-5}\) maps them all very close to 0.5, which would compress rather than rescale the physically meaningful pressure variation unless an affine scaling is applied first. The paper also does not say whether the MSE is computed before or after inverse normalization, nor how the sigmoid transformation handles pressure sign and the arbitrary additive constant often present in incompressible flow pressure. This is a reproducibility issue and may affect interpretation of the reported very small MSE.

comments.title: Claims of learning shocks, boundary conditions, and governing rules are stronger than the evidence shown
comments.quote: our empirical results (see Figure 4) show that the Neural-PDE is able to learn the shock waves, boundary conditions and the rules of the equation, and predict _u_ and _v_ simultaneously with an overall MSE of 1 _._ 4018 _×_ 10 _[−]_[5] .
comments.explanation: The reported low MSE and visual agreement are encouraging, especially for a nonlinear hyperbolic problem. However, the evidence described here shows accurate prediction on data generated by an upwind finite-difference solver under a particular setup; it does not by itself establish that the model has learned the PDE rules, boundary conditions, or shock physics in a transferable sense. For shocks in particular, global MSE can be small even when discontinuity location or jump structure is imperfect, and boundary behavior should ideally be evaluated separately. A more cautious phrasing would be that the model accurately reproduces the solver-generated solution on the tested cases.

comments.title: Numerical solver output is described as an exact solution
comments.quote: We use a upwinding finite difference scheme to create the training data
comments.explanation: The use of a finite-difference solver as reference data is standard and appropriate for this kind of supervised experiment. The issue is that the passage later refers to the displayed reference as the “exact solution,” while the data are apparently generated by an upwind numerical scheme. Unless an analytic solution is actually used for comparison, this is more accurately a numerical reference solution or ground truth dataset. The distinction matters for inviscid Burgers’ equations because shocks and numerical diffusion make the solver choice part of the target being learned.

comments.title: Overbroad claim about deep learning efficiently solving high-dimensional nonlinear problems
comments.quote: The state-of-the-art researches have shown the promising power of deep learning in solving highdimensional nonlinear problems in engineering, biology and finance with efficiency in computation and accuracy in prediction.
comments.explanation: I read this as a high-level concluding statement rather than a claim proved by the experiments in this paper, and the cited literature in the introduction does include deep-learning approaches for high-dimensional PDEs and applied scientific problems. However, the wording is stronger than what the paper itself or the literature can generally support. The paper’s own experiments are primarily 1D/2D grid-based PDE examples, plus a 2D coupled fluid system, so they do not demonstrate scalable solution of genuinely high-dimensional nonlinear PDEs. In the broader field, deep learning has shown promise for selected high-dimensional problems, but efficiency and accuracy are usually problem-dependent and often lack rigorous guarantees. A more careful phrasing would qualify this as “has shown promise” rather than implying that state-of-the-art methods have broadly solved such problems efficiently and accurately.

comments.title: The absence of stability and convergence theory is stated too broadly
comments.quote: Due to the high nonlinearity of the neural network system and the curse of dimensionality (Hutzenthaler et al., 2019), theorems guiding stability and convergence of solutions predicted by the neural network are to be revealed.
comments.explanation: The authors are correctly identifying an important open issue for their proposed Neural-PDE framework: unlike classical finite-difference/finite-element schemes, a learned recurrent predictor generally does not come with CFL-type stability conditions, consistency/convergence theory, or long-time error guarantees. That concern is well taken. However, the statement is too sweeping as written. Even around the time of the cited work, there were theoretical results for certain neural-network PDE solvers, approximation rates, and convergence in restricted settings; by 2026 this is even more true for PINNs, Deep Ritz-type methods, neural operators, and semilinear parabolic PDE approximations. Moreover, the cited Hutzenthaler et al. paper is specifically about rectified deep networks overcoming the curse of dimensionality for semilinear heat equations, so invoking it as if it simply explains the absence of theory may confuse readers. The unresolved gap is more specifically the lack of a stability/convergence theory for this particular data-driven BiLSTM Neural-PDE time-advancement method, especially for nonlinear PDE systems and out-of-distribution/long-horizon prediction.
